label: Uncomplicated hives without anaphylaxis or mucosal lesions
found: Your clinician diagnosed hives and did not find anaphylaxis, mucosal lesions, infection, or another emergency problem today.
home: Avoid the trigger if one was identified.
Use cool compresses if they help itching.
Do not scratch the rash if you can avoid it.
meds: Take antihistamines or other medicines only as prescribed or recommended.
Do not take sedating medicine before driving or working if it makes you sleepy.
return: Trouble breathing, throat or tongue swelling, fainting, vomiting with rash, or symptoms involving more than one body system.
Skin peeling, mouth sores, target lesions, fever, or feeling very ill.
Hives that keep returning, worsen quickly, or do not improve as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, allergy, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated hives or urticaria.
No anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
exclusion: Anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, mucosal lesions, skin peeling, target lesions, systemic illness, infection concern, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports hives framing, trigger avoidance, antihistamine use, and emergency warning signs for breathing or throat symptoms. Autonomous expansion approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated hives without anaphylaxis, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed allergy plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated hives or urticaria
no anaphylaxis, airway symptoms, mucosal lesions, systemic illness, or specialist-directed allergy plan documented

label: Uncomplicated common cold without pneumonia or breathing red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated common cold.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a common cold and did not find pneumonia, breathing distress, or another emergency problem today.
home: Rest and drink fluids as tolerated.
Use humidified air, saline spray, honey if safe for you, or other comfort measures if helpful.
Wash your hands and avoid close contact while symptoms are contagious.
meds: Antibiotics are not needed for an uncomplicated common cold.
Use over-the-counter medicines only if safe for you and follow the label.
return: Trouble breathing, chest pain, blue lips, confusion, fainting, or worsening shortness of breath.
Fever that is high or persistent, dehydration, or symptoms that get much worse after starting to improve.
Concern for pneumonia, asthma/COPD flare, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated common cold.
No pneumonia, hypoxia, asthma/COPD exacerbation, severe dehydration, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Pneumonia, hypoxia, shortness of breath at rest, chest pain, asthma/COPD exacerbation, severe dehydration, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus and AHRQ support common-cold self-care and antibiotic avoidance while preserving pneumonia and breathing red flags. Autonomous expansion approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed uncomplicated common cold without pneumonia, hypoxia, breathing distress, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated common cold
no pneumonia, hypoxia, breathing distress, severe dehydration, or specialist-directed plan documented

label: Localized common wart without genital or immunocompromised-host red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit a localized common wart.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a localized common wart and did not find infection, genital involvement, or another emergency problem today.
home: Do not pick, cut, or dig at the wart.
Keep it covered if it is irritated or likely to spread.
Avoid sharing items that rub the area.
meds: Use wart treatment only if your clinician recommended it.
Stop any treatment that causes severe pain, spreading redness, or an open wound.
return: Spreading redness, pus, fever, severe pain, or the area turns black.
The wart is on the genitals, near the eye, or you are immunocompromised.
Rapid growth, bleeding, diagnostic uncertainty, or symptoms not improving as expected.
follow: Follow up with primary care, dermatology, urgent care, or the ED if symptoms are not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized common wart.
No genital wart/STI pathway, near-eye lesion, infection, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or diagnostic uncertainty.
exclusion: Genital wart/STI pathway, near-eye lesion, infection concern, immunocompromised host, rapidly enlarging/bleeding lesion, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed dermatology plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports wart framing, spread prevention, and care escalation for painful, infected, or unusual lesions. Autonomous expansion approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed localized common wart without genital/STI pathway, infection, immunocompromised host, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or diagnostic uncertainty.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of localized common wart
localized skin wart with no infection, immune-risk context, diagnostic uncertainty, or specialist-directed plan documented

label: Forearm contusion without fracture or neurovascular red flags
summary: Your symptoms fit an uncomplicated forearm bruise.
found: Your clinician diagnosed a forearm bruise. We did not find a fracture, circulation problem, nerve problem, or another emergency problem today.
home: Rest the sore area as instructed.
Use ice or elevation if recommended.
Avoid activity that clearly worsens the pain until you are improving.
meds: Use pain medicine only if prescribed or recommended, and follow the label.
Avoid NSAIDs if your clinician told you not to take them.
return: Worsening severe pain, rapidly increasing swelling, numbness, weakness, color change, or trouble using the area.
New deformity, concern for a broken bone, or pain not improving as expected.
Easy bleeding, a very large bruise, fever, or new symptoms that worry you.
follow: Follow up with primary care, orthopedics, urgent care, or the ED if pain is not improving or your clinician told you to recheck.
inclusion: Adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated forearm contusion or bruise.
No fracture/dislocation concern, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, bleeding-risk plan, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
exclusion: Fracture or dislocation concern, major trauma, open wound, neurovascular compromise, severe pain, compartment concern, or inability to use the area.
Anticoagulation/bleeding disorder requiring a separate plan, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed orthopedic plan.
source_audit_notes: MedlinePlus supports bruise/contusion home care and warning signs for worsening swelling, pain, and unexplained bruising. Autonomous expansion approved this narrow v1 only for clinician-diagnosed forearm contusion without fracture/dislocation concern, neurovascular compromise, bleeding-risk plan, pregnancy/pediatric pathway, or specialist-directed plan.
required_modifiers: adult with clinician diagnosis or discharge impression of uncomplicated forearm contusion or bruise
no fracture/dislocation concern, neurovascular compromise, bleeding-risk plan, or specialist-directed plan documented
